audioscript.text: Section 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear a telephone conversation between a woman who wants to attend a cookery class, and an official at a tourist information center.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 1 to 6.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will see that there is an example that has been done for you.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: On this occasion only, the conversation relating to this will be played first.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Hello, Tourist Information Center.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: Mike speaking.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: How can I help you?
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: Oh, hi.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I wanted to find out about cookery classes.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I believe there are some one-day classes for tourists.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Well, they're open to everyone, but tourists are always welcome.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: OK, let me give you some details of what's available.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: There are several classes, one very popular one is at the Food Studio.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: The first cooking class is at the Food Studio, so studio has been written in the space.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now we shall begin.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You should answer the questions as you listen, because you will not hear the recording a second time.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Listen carefully, and answer questions 1 to 6.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Hello, Tourist Information Center.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: Mike speaking.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: How can I help you?
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: Oh, hi.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I wanted to find out about cookery classes.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I believe there are some one-day classes for tourists.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Well, they're open to everyone, but tourists are always welcome.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: OK, let me give you some details of what's available.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: There are several classes, one very popular one is at the Food Studio.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: OK, they focus on seasonal products, and as well as teaching you how to cook them, they also show you how to choose them.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: Right, that sounds good.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: How big are the classes?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I'm not sure exactly, but they'll be quite small.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: And could I get a private lesson there?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I think so.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: Let me check.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: Yes, they do offer those.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: Though in fact most of the people who attend the classes find it's a nice way of getting to know one another.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: I suppose it must be, yes.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: And this company has a special deal for clients,
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: where they offer a discount of 20 percent, if you return for a further class.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: OK, but you said there were several classes.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: That's right.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: Another one you might be interested in is Bond's Cookery School.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: They're quite new.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: They just opened 6 months ago, but I've heard good things about them.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: They concentrate on teaching you to prepare healthy food, and they have quite a lot of specialist staff.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: So is that food for people on a diet, and things like that?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I don't know if I'd be interested in that.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Well, I don't think they particularly focus on low calorie diets or weight loss.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: It's more to do with recipes that look at specific needs, like including ingredients that will help build up your bones and make them stronger, that sort of thing.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: I see.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Well, I might be interested, I'm not sure.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Do they have a website I could check?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Yes, just key in the name of the school, it'll come up.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: And if you want to know more about them, every Thursday evening they have a lecture at the school.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: It's free, and you don't need to book or anything, just turn up at 7:30.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: And that might give you an idea of whether you want to go to an actual class.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the conversation, you have some time to look at question 7 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 7 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: OK, there's one more place you might be interested in.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: That's got a rather strange name.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: It's called The Arretsa Center.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: That's spelled ARRETSA.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: OK, they've got a very good reputation.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: They do a bit of meat and fish cookery, but they mostly specialize in vegetarian dishes.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: That's certainly an area I'd like to learn more about.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I've got lots of friends who don't eat meat.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: In fact, I think I might have seen that school today.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Is it just by the market?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: That's right.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: So they don't have any problem getting their ingredients.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: They're right next door.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: And they also offer a special two-hour course in how to use a knife.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: They cover all the different skills, buying them, sharpening, chopping techniques.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: It gets booked up quickly though, so you'd need to check it was available.
audioscript.speaker: OFFICIAL

audioscript.text: Right, well, thank you very much.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I'll go and check that out.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Section 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear the chairman of the Highways Committee of Granford speaking to members of the public about proposed changes to traffic and parking in the area.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 11 to 13.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully, and answer questions 11 to 13.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Good evening everyone.
audioscript.speaker: PHIL SUTTON

audioscript.text: My name is Phil Sutton, and I'm chairman of the Highways Committee.
audioscript.speaker: PHIL SUTTON

audioscript.text: We've called this meeting to inform members of the public about the new regulations for traffic and parking we're proposing for Granford.
audioscript.speaker: PHIL SUTTON

audioscript.text: I'll start by summarizing these changes before we open the meeting to questions.
audioscript.speaker: PHIL SUTTON

audioscript.text: So, why do we need to make these changes to traffic systems in Granford?
audioscript.speaker: PHIL SUTTON

audioscript.text: Well, we're very aware that traffic is becoming an increasing problem.
audioscript.speaker: PHIL SUTTON

audioscript.text: It's been especially noticeable with the increase in heavy traffic while they've been building the new hospital.
audioscript.speaker: PHIL SUTTON

audioscript.text: But it's the overall rise in the volume of traffic of all kinds that's concerning us.
audioscript.speaker: PHIL SUTTON

audioscript.text: To date, there's not been any increase in traffic accidents.
audioscript.speaker: PHIL SUTTON

audioscript.text: But that's not something we want to see happen, obviously.
audioscript.speaker: PHIL SUTTON

audioscript.text: Uh. We recently carried out a survey of local residents, and their responses were interesting.
audioscript.speaker: PHIL SUTTON

audioscript.text: People were very concerned about the lack of visibility on some roads due to cars parked along the sides of the roads, we'd expected complaints about the congestion near the school, when parents are dropping off their children or picking them up.
audioscript.speaker: PHIL SUTTON

audioscript.text: But this wasn't top of the list, and nor were noise and fumes from trucks and lorries, though they were mentioned by some people.
audioscript.speaker: PHIL SUTTON

audioscript.text: We think these new traffic regulations would make a lot of difference, but we still have a long way to go.
audioscript.speaker: PHIL SUTTON

audioscript.text: We've managed to keep our proposals within budget, just.
audioscript.speaker: PHIL SUTTON

audioscript.text: So they can be covered by the Council, but of course it's no good introducing new regulations if we don't have a way of making sure that everyone obeys them.
audioscript.speaker: PHIL SUTTON

audioscript.text: And that's an area we're still working on with the help of representatives from the police force.
audioscript.speaker: PHIL SUTTON

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the talk, you have some time to look at questions 14 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 14 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: OK, so this slide shows a map of the central area of Granford, with the High Street in the middle and School Road on the right.
audioscript.speaker: PHIL SUTTON

audioscript.text: Now we already have a set of traffic lights in the High Street at the junction with Station Road, but we're planning to have another set at the other end at the School Road junction.
audioscript.speaker: PHIL SUTTON

audioscript.text: To regulate the flow of traffic along the High Street.
audioscript.speaker: PHIL SUTTON

audioscript.text: We've decided we definitely need a pedestrian crossing.
audioscript.speaker: PHIL SUTTON

audioscript.text: We considered putting this on School Road just outside the school.
audioscript.speaker: PHIL SUTTON

audioscript.text: But in the end, we decided that could lead to a lot of traffic congestion.
audioscript.speaker: PHIL SUTTON

audioscript.text: So we decided to locate it on the High Street, crossing the road in front of the supermarket.
audioscript.speaker: PHIL SUTTON

audioscript.text: That's a very busy area, so it should help things there.
audioscript.speaker: PHIL SUTTON

audioscript.text: We are proposing some changes to parking.
audioscript.speaker: PHIL SUTTON

audioscript.text: At present, parking isn't allowed on the High Street outside the library.
audioscript.speaker: PHIL SUTTON

audioscript.text: But we are going to change that and allow parking there, but not at the other end of the High Street near School Road.
audioscript.speaker: PHIL SUTTON

audioscript.text: There'll be a new no parking sign on School Road.
audioscript.speaker: PHIL SUTTON

audioscript.text: Just by the entrance to the school, forbidding parking for 25 meters.
audioscript.speaker: PHIL SUTTON

audioscript.text: This should improve visibility for drivers and pedestrians, especially on the bend just to the north of the school.
audioscript.speaker: PHIL SUTTON

audioscript.text: As far as disabled drivers are concerned, at present, they have parking outside the supermarket, but lorries also use those spaces, so we've got two new disabled parking spaces on the side road.
audioscript.speaker: PHIL SUTTON

audioscript.text: Up towards the bank.
audioscript.speaker: PHIL SUTTON

audioscript.text: It's not ideal, but probably better than the present arrangement.
audioscript.speaker: PHIL SUTTON

audioscript.text: We also plan to widen the pavement on School Road.
audioscript.speaker: PHIL SUTTON

audioscript.text: We think we can manage to get an extra half-meter on the bend just before you get to the school on the same side of the road.
audioscript.speaker: PHIL SUTTON

audioscript.text: Finally, we've introduced new restrictions on loading and unloading for the supermarket, so lorries will only be allowed to stop there before 8 am. That's the supermarket on School Road.
audioscript.speaker: PHIL SUTTON

audioscript.text: We kept to the existing arrangements with the High Street supermarket.
audioscript.speaker: PHIL SUTTON

audioscript.text: OK, so that's about it.
audioscript.speaker: PHIL SUTTON

audioscript.text: Now, would anyone...
audioscript.speaker: PHIL SUTTON

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: But we are going to change that and allow parking there, but not at the other end of the High Street near School Road.
utterances.speaker: PHIL SUTTON

utterances.text: There'll be a new no parking sign on School Road.
utterances.speaker: PHIL SUTTON

utterances.text: Just by the entrance to the school, forbidding parking for 25 meters.
utterances.speaker: PHIL SUTTON

utterances.text: This should improve visibility for drivers and pedestrians, especially on the bend just to the north of the school.
utterances.speaker: PHIL SUTTON

utterances.text: As far as disabled drivers are concerned, at present, they have parking outside the supermarket, but lorries also use those spaces, so we've got two new disabled parking spaces on the side road.
utterances.speaker: PHIL SUTTON

utterances.text: Up towards the bank.
utterances.speaker: PHIL SUTTON

utterances.text: It's not ideal, but probably better than the present arrangement.
utterances.speaker: PHIL SUTTON

utterances.text: We also plan to widen the pavement on School Road.
utterances.speaker: PHIL SUTTON

utterances.text: We think we can manage to get an extra half-meter on the bend just before you get to the school on the same side of the road.
utterances.speaker: PHIL SUTTON

utterances.text: Finally, we've introduced new restrictions on loading and unloading for the supermarket, so lorries will only be allowed to stop there before 8 am. That's the supermarket on School Road.
utterances.speaker: PHIL SUTTON

utterances.text: We kept to the existing arrangements with the High Street supermarket.
utterances.speaker: PHIL SUTTON

utterances.text: OK, so that's about it.
utterances.speaker: PHIL SUTTON

utterances.text: Now, would anyone...
utterances.speaker: PHIL SUTTON

utterances.text: That is the end of section 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items.title: Why are changes needed to traffic systems in Granford?
options: The number of traffic accidents has risen.
The amount of traffic on the roads has increased.
The types of vehicles on the roads have changed.

items.title: In a survey, local residents particularly complained about
options: dangerous driving by parents.
pollution from trucks and lorries.
inconvenience from parked cars.

items.title: According to the speaker, one problem with the new regulations will be
options: raising money to pay for them.
finding a way to make people follow them.
getting the support of the police.

audioscript.text: Section 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear two biology students called Emma and Jack discussing an experiment they are going to do together.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 21 to 25.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 21 to 25.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: We've got to choose a topic for our experiment, haven't we, Jack?
audioscript.speaker: EMMA

audioscript.text: Were you thinking of something to do with seeds?
audioscript.speaker: EMMA

audioscript.text: Hmm, that's right.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: I thought we could look at seed germination, how a seed begins to grow.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: OK, any particular reason?
audioscript.speaker: EMMA

audioscript.text: I know you're hoping to work in plant science eventually.
audioscript.speaker: EMMA

audioscript.text: Yeah, but practically everything we do is going to feed into that.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: No, there's an optional module on seed structure and function in the 3rd year that I might do, so I thought it might be useful for that.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: If I choose that option, I don't have to do a dissertation module.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: Good idea.
audioscript.speaker: EMMA

audioscript.text: Hmm, well I thought for this experiment, we could look at the relationship between seed size and the way the seeds are planted.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: So we could plant different sized seeds in different ways and see which grow best.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: OK, we'd need to allow time for the seeds to come up.
audioscript.speaker: EMMA

audioscript.text: That should be fine if we start now.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: A lot of the other possible experiments need quite a bit longer.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: So that would make it a good one to choose.
audioscript.speaker: EMMA

audioscript.text: And I don't suppose it needs much equipment.
audioscript.speaker: EMMA

audioscript.text: We're not doing chemical analysis or anything, though that's not really an issue.
audioscript.speaker: EMMA

audioscript.text: We've got plenty of equipment in the laboratory.
audioscript.speaker: EMMA

audioscript.text: Yeah, we need to have a word with the tutor if we're going to go ahead with it though.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: I'm sure our aim's OK, it's not very ambitious, but the assignment's only 10% of our final mark, isn't it?
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: But we need to be sure we're the only ones doing it.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: Yeah, it's only 5% actually, but it would be a bit boring if everyone was doing it.
audioscript.speaker: EMMA

audioscript.text: Did you read that book on seed germination on our reading list?
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: The one by Graves?
audioscript.speaker: EMMA

audioscript.text: Hmm, I looked through it for my last experiment, though it wasn't all that relevant there.
audioscript.speaker: EMMA

audioscript.text: It would be for this experiment though, I found it quite hard to follow, lots about the theory which I hadn't expected.
audioscript.speaker: EMMA

audioscript.text: Yes, I'd been hoping for something more practical.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: It does include references to the recent findings on genetically modified seeds, though.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: Yes, that was interesting.
audioscript.speaker: EMMA

audioscript.text: I read an article about seed germination by Lee Hall.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: About seeds that lie in the ground for ages and only germinate after a fire.
audioscript.speaker: EMMA

audioscript.text: Hmm, that's the one.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: I knew a bit about it already, but not about this research.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: His analysis of figures comparing the times of the fires and the proportion of seeds that germinated was done in a lot of detail, very impressive.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: Was that the article with the illustrations of early stages of plant development?
audioscript.speaker: EMMA

audioscript.text: They were very clear.
audioscript.speaker: EMMA

audioscript.text: I think those diagrams were in another article.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the discussion, you have some time to look at questions 26 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 26 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Anyway, shall we have a look at the procedure for our experiment?
audioscript.speaker: EMMA

audioscript.text: We'll need to get going with it quite soon.
audioscript.speaker: EMMA

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: So the first thing we have to do is find our seeds.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: I think vegetable seeds would be best, and obviously they mustn't all be the same size.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: So how many sorts do we need?
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: About four different ones.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: I think that would be enough.
audioscript.speaker: EMMA

audioscript.text: There'll be quite a large number of seeds for each one.
audioscript.speaker: EMMA

audioscript.text: Then for each seed, we need to find out how much it weighs.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: And also measure its dimensions, and we need to keep a careful record of all that.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: That'll be quite time consuming, and we also need to decide how deep we're going to plant the seeds.
audioscript.speaker: EMMA

audioscript.text: Right on the surface a few millimeters down, or several centimeters.
audioscript.speaker: EMMA

audioscript.text: OK, so then we get planting.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: Do you think we can plant several seeds together in the same plant pot?
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: No, I think we need a different one for each seed.
audioscript.speaker: EMMA

audioscript.text: Hmm, right, and we'll need to label them.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: We can use different colored labels.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: Then we wait for the seeds to germinate.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: I reckon that'll be about 3 weeks, depending on what the weather's like.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: Then we see if our plants have come up.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: And write down how tall they've grown.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: Then all we have to do is look at our numbers and see if there's any relation between them.
audioscript.speaker: EMMA

audioscript.text: That's right.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: So then we...
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: That is the end of Section 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items.title: Why is Jack interested in investigating seed germination?
options: He may do a module on a related topic later on.
He wants to have a career in plant science.
He is thinking of choosing this topic for his dissertation.

items.title: Jack and Emma agree the main advantage of their present experiment is that it can be
options: described very easily.
carried out inside the laboratory.
completed in the time available.

items.title: What do they decide to check with their tutor?
options: whether their aim is appropriate
whether anyone else has chosen this topic
whether the assignment contributes to their final grade

items.title: They agree that Graves' book on seed germination is disappointing because
options: it fails to cover recent advances in seed science.
the content is irrelevant for them.
its focus is very theoretical.

items.title: What does Jack say about the article on seed germination by Lee Hall?
options: The diagrams of plant development are useful.
The analysis of seed germination statistics is thorough.
The findings on seed germination after fires are surprising.

items: Select seeds of different <input> and sizes.
Measure and record the <input> and size of each one.
Decide on the <input> to be used.
Use a different <input> for each seed and label it.
After about 3 weeks, record the plant's <input>.
body.title: Stages in the experiment

test_text.title: Effect of urban environments on animals

audioscript.text: Section 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear part of a lecture on an environmental studies course about recent research on how animals are adapting to urban environments.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully, and answer questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Hi. Today we're going to be looking at animals in urban environments, and I'm going to be telling you about some research on how they're affected by these environments.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Now, in evolutionary terms, urban environments represent huge upheavals, the sorts of massive changes that usually happen over millions of years.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And we used to think that only a few species could adapt to this new environment.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: One species which is well known as being highly adaptable is the crow.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And there have been various studies about how they manage to learn new skills.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Another successful species is the pigeon.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Because they're able to perch on ledges on the walls of city buildings, just like they once perched on cliffs by the sea, but in fact we're now finding that these early immigrants were just the start of a more general movement of animals into cities.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And of adaptation by these animals to city life.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And one thing that researchers are finding especially interesting is the speed with which they're doing this.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We're not talking about gradual evolution here.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: These animals are changing fast.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Let me tell you about some of the studies that have been carried out in this area.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So, in the University of Minnesota, a biologist called Emilie Snell-Rood.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And her colleagues looked at specimens of urbanized small mammals such as mice and gophers that had been collected in Minnesota, and that are now kept in museums there.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And she looked at specimens that had been collected over the last hundred years, which is a very short time in evolutionary terms.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And she found that during that time, these small mammals had experienced a jump in brain size when compared to rural mammals.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Now we can't be sure this means they're more intelligent.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But since the sizes of other parts of the body didn't change, it does suggest that something cognitive was going on.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And Snell-Rood thinks that this change might reflect the cognitive demands of adjusting to city life.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Having to look in different places to find food for example, and coping with a whole new set of dangers.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Then over in Germany at the Max Planck Institute, there's another biologist called Catarina Miranda, who's done some experiments with blackbirds living in urban and rural areas.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: She's been looking not at their anatomy, but at their behavior.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So as you might expect, she's found that the urban blackbirds tend to be quite bold.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: They're prepared to face up to a lot of threats that would frighten away their country counterparts, but there's one type of situation that does seem to frighten the urban blackbirds.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And that's anything new, anything they haven't experienced before.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And if you think about it, that's quite sensible for a bird living in the city.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Jonathan Atwell in Indiana University is looking at how a range of animals respond to urban environments.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: He's found that when they're under stress, their endocrine systems react by reducing the amount of hormones such as corticosterone into their blood.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It's a sensible-seeming adaptation.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: A rat that gets scared every time a subway train rolls past won't be very successful.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: There's just one more study I'd like to mention, which is by Sarah Partan and her team.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And they've been looking at how squirrels communicate in an urban environment.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And they found that a routine part of their communication is carried out by waving their tails.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: You do also see this in the country, but it's much more prevalent in cities.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Possibly because it's effective in a noisy environment.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So what are the long-term implications of this?
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: One possibility is that we may see completely new species developing in cities, but on the other hand, it's possible that not all of these adaptations will be permanent.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Once the animals got accustomed to its new environment, it may no longer need the features it's developed.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So now we've had a look...
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of Section 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Having to look in different places to find food for example, and coping with a whole new set of dangers.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Then over in Germany at the Max Planck Institute, there's another biologist called Catarina Miranda, who's done some experiments with blackbirds living in urban and rural areas.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: She's been looking not at their anatomy, but at their behavior.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So as you might expect, she's found that the urban blackbirds tend to be quite bold.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: They're prepared to face up to a lot of threats that would frighten away their country counterparts, but there's one type of situation that does seem to frighten the urban blackbirds.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And that's anything new, anything they haven't experienced before.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And if you think about it, that's quite sensible for a bird living in the city.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Jonathan Atwell in Indiana University is looking at how a range of animals respond to urban environments.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: He's found that when they're under stress, their endocrine systems react by reducing the amount of hormones such as corticosterone into their blood.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It's a sensible-seeming adaptation.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: A rat that gets scared every time a subway train rolls past won't be very successful.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: There's just one more study I'd like to mention, which is by Sarah Partan and her team.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And they've been looking at how squirrels communicate in an urban environment.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And they found that a routine part of their communication is carried out by waving their tails.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: You do also see this in the country, but it's much more prevalent in cities.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Possibly because it's effective in a noisy environment.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So what are the long-term implications of this?
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: One possibility is that we may see completely new species developing in cities, but on the other hand, it's possible that not all of these adaptations will be permanent.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Once the animals got accustomed to its new environment, it may no longer need the features it's developed.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So now we've had a look...
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That is the end of Section 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

body.title: Effects of urban environments on animals
